definition: Non-GAAP earnings practices systematically understate equity portfolio duration by 30-40%. S&P 500 GAAP EPS (~$200-210) vs non-GAAP (~$250-260) shows a ~25% gap. At GAAP P/E of ~25-27x, approximately 55-65% of present value sits in terminal value with duration sensitivity of ~15-20% per 100bp. At non-GAAP P/E of ~21x, terminal value share drops to ~45-50% and duration sensitivity to ~12-15%.
evidence: GAAP EPS ~$200-210 vs non-GAAP ~$250-260, approximately 25% gap
GAAP P/E ~25-27x implies 55-65% terminal value share; non-GAAP P/E ~21x implies 45-50%
Duration sensitivity difference: ~15-20% vs ~12-15% per 100bp rate change
Debate pair_1: 'the single most original contribution across both analyses'
At GAAP multiples (26-29x), 55-65% of PV in terminal value with ~15-20% sensitivity per 100bp
At non-GAAP multiples (21-22x), 45-50% terminal value with ~12-15% sensitivity
Direction 'almost certainly correct' but 30-40% magnitude is author's construction
open_questions: How much of the GAAP/non-GAAP gap is SBC (secularly growing) vs one-time items?
Do investors actually use non-GAAP multiples for rate sensitivity analysis, or is this a measurement issue without behavioral consequence?
Is the 30-40% understatement estimate robust to different discount rate assumptions?
Needs wider empirical study — magnitude is author's construction not widely validated
Whether risk models will incorporate this adjustment
